infons.title: Keywords

passages.text: How deep resolve affects the stability of texture features for different MR image acceleration methods - a phantom and a human brain study
Objectives

This study evaluates the stability of texture features in MRI reconstructed with and without a vendor-provided deep learning method across different acceleration techniques, including GRAPPA and SMS. The aim is to assess how such reconstructions influence the preservation of diagnostically relevant image textures in phantom and brain scans.
Methods

MRI scans were performed on a 1.5T system using a fluid-filled phantom and healthy volunteers. T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences were acquired with varying GRAPPA, SMS, and Deep Resolve settings. A total of 570 texture features were extracted from regions of interest using MaZda software. Stability was evaluated using Lin’s concordance correlation coefficient and the Wilcoxon rank-sum test.
Results

In phantom data, high concordance between Deep Resolve and standard reconstruction was rare (0–2 features with Lin > 0.9), and the number of features with Lin > 0.8 decreased with higher acceleration. SMS2 and GRAPPA R = 2 preserved the largest number of features (up to 27–151 with Lin > 0.8). In brain images, 36–414 features achieved Lin > 0.8 depending on tissue and acceleration, with cerebrospinal fluid and white matter showing greater concordance than cortex. Deep Resolve also produced a higher number of features meeting the Wilcoxon criterion for equal medians (p ≥ 0.05), particularly under moderate acceleration (e.g., up to 486 features in cortex for GRAPPA R = 2).
Conclusion

Deep Resolve modifies MRI texture features in a manner that depends on tissue type and acceleration factor. The highest texture stability was observed under moderate acceleration, while higher acceleration consistently reduced concordance. These findings highlight the need to account for reconstruction effects when using texture-based or radiomic analysis.
Supplementary Information
The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12880-026-02317-z.
Introduction

Over the past decade, Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) has become a central tool in diagnostic and therapeutic medicine. The non-invasive nature of MRI and the increasing availability of advanced hardware have contributed to its growing impact in practical medicine. Despite its numerous advantages, a limitation of MRI is its relatively long image acquisition time. As a result, recent acceleration efforts have focused on developing shorter imaging protocols that maintain or improve image quality without prolonging scan durations. Several advanced techniques have been developed to reduce MRI acquisition time while preserving image quality. Among these, parallel imaging methods such as Sensitivity Encoding (SENSE), Simultaneous Multi-Slice (SMS), and Generalized Autocalibrating Partially Parallel Acquisitions (GRAPPA) are of particular importance. SENSE operates in the image domain, whereas both GRAPPA and SMS are based on k-space data processing. Notably, SMS is typically used in combination with either SENSE or GRAPPA to facilitate unaliasing, enhance signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and achieve higher acceleration factors. The development of Convolutional Neural Networks, combined with their acceleration on GPU architectures, has played a pivotal role in advancing methods for reconstructing high-quality images from under-sampled or noisy MRI data. A prominent example is Deep Resolve (DR, Siemens), which enables signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) enhancement, resolution improvement, and image denoising.

DR can be applied as post-processing after GRAPPA or synergistically with SMS, improving k-space results. It relies on a model trained on a large MR dataset to capture structural and textural patterns essential for high-fidelity reconstruction. The primary benefit recognized by both clinicians and patients is the reduction in scanning time without a corresponding loss in image quality. In contrast, previously employed acceleration techniques often compromise image quality, necessitating a trade-off between imaging speed and diagnostic fidelity. Although DR represents a highly promising technique, certain limitations have been identified. Subtle pathologies may be obscured in the reconstructed images due to excessive smoothing-a known effect of denoising algorithms that can lead to the loss of true signal. Another concern is learned image bias, where anatomical details not represented in the training dataset may be suppressed or entirely omitted from the final image. Radiologists and physicists, but especially radiographers, must balance noise reduction with diagnostic accuracy, and validation against ground truth or fully sampled reference images remains essential for reliable reconstruction.

Texture analysis provides a quantitative method to assess spatial organization and variability of pixel intensities. Unlike conventional measures such as mean intensity or contrast, texture analysis captures the spatial relationships and recurring structures inherent in the image data. Common features include homogeneity, contrast, entropy, coarseness, and directionality. Texture features serve as powerful and distinctive tools for assessing the preservation of MR image quality, as the fine textures of imaged tissues are often the first to be lost or distorted. Texture analysis can detect subtle, clinically relevant changes that are not captured by conventional metrics such as SNR or peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), which fail to reflect local image structure. This includes the identification of noise or excessive smoothing, both of which may directly affect the preservation of anatomical details. Texture analysis quantitatively mimics human perception of image detail, thereby bridging the gap between subjective visual assessment and objective mathematical evaluation. Furthermore, its reference-free nature makes it a universally applicable tool for image quality analysis.

The aim of this study was to evaluate preservation of textural features in phantom and in vivo reconstructions, using non-parametric and comparative approaches to assess the impact of deep learning-based reconstruction methods on image formation.
Methods

The study protocol was designed in accordance with guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki and the Good Clinical Practice Declaration Statement. Written approval was obtained from the Bioethics Committee to conduct this study (No. 11/KBL/OIL/2025 dated 11 March, 2025 and 03 March 2026). Axial T2-weighted and FLAIR images of a spherical phantom and of the brains of four volunteers were acquired. As summarized in Table 1, all imaging configurations, including those with GRAPPA and SMS acceleration, were acquired both with and without the vendor’s Deep Resolve reconstruction pipeline, which consisted of Deep Resolve Gain and Deep Resolve Sharp. These datasets were compared against the corresponding standard Siemens reconstruction (STD), which serves as the baseline without deep-learning enhancement.

Summary of all image acquisition and reconstruction combinations used in the brain and the phantom study. Each configuration was assessed with and without Deep Resolve applied. GRAPPA was evaluated at acceleration factors R = 2, 3, and 4, while SMS was tested at slice acceleration factors of 2, 3, and 4

MR data were acquired on a 1.5T Siemens Altea (Erlangen, Germany) with 16-channel coils, 45 mT/m gradients, and 145 T/m/s slew rate. Acquisition parameters are listed in Table 2, with an extended version provided in the Supplementary Material.
Summary of fixed and variable acquisition parameters used in the phantom imaging protocol

A standard fluid-filled spherical phantom was used to assess MRI hardware under head imaging conditions. It consisted of a non-ferromagnetic acrylic shell with a diameter of approximately 170 mm, resembling the average size of the skull. The wall thickness of the shell was about 5 mm, ensuring structural integrity while maintaining RF transparency. The internal volume was filled with a conductive solution of distilled water doped with nickel sulfate (NiSO₄) and sodium chloride (NaCl), prepared by dissolving 3.75 g of NiSO₄·6 H₂O and 5 g of NaCl in 1000 g of distilled water. This composition results in a conductivity of approximately 0.5–0.6 S/m at 1.5 T and provides tissue-mimicking relaxation properties, with T1 relaxation times of approximately 1000–1200 ms and T2 relaxation times of around 80–100 ms at 1.5 T, according to manufacturer reference specifications. Scanning was performed at room temperature (20–22 °C) to maintain stable relaxation times. These phantoms are widely employed for head-coil validation and MRI performance checks, as recommended in standardized QA protocols. Scan times ranged from 55 s (with maximal acceleration) to 157 s (without acceleration). As illustrated in Fig. 1, each acceleration setting was acquired both with and without Deep Resolve. After positioning the spherical phantom inside the head coil, the acquisition was preceded by a stabilization period of up to 10 min to allow the internal fluid to reach thermal and mechanical equilibrium.
Acquisition times for different acceleration techniques (GRAPPA, SMS) with and without Deep Resolve reconstruction

All datasets contained 12-bit TIFF images, with a matrix size of 392 × 480 pixels. Representative T2-weighted phantom images and their intensity histograms, together with reconstructions after GRAPPA, SMS, conventional, and DR methods, are shown in Fig. 2. Representative in-vivo T2-weighted brain images reconstructed with standard and Deep Resolve methods for the corresponding GRAPPA and SMS acceleration settings are presented in Fig. 3. DICOM images were converted to TIFF using SimpleITK (v2.4.1). No additional windowing, filtering, or intensity rescaling was applied, and the original scanner intensity range was preserved.

Representative T2 phantom cross-sections and their corresponding intensity histograms (30 bins). The top row (“Standard”) and the bottom row (“Deep Resolve”) display results across all acceleration techniques, including None, GRAPPA 2–4, and SMS 2–4

Representative in-vivo T2-weighted axial brain images reconstructed with the standard method (top row) and Deep Resolve (bottom row). The left column shows images without acceleration, followed by images acquired with GRAPPA and SMS acceleration

In all images, background intensity ended near 40 (Fig. 2b). Thus, a threshold of 51 was applied to segment the phantom (Fig. 4a), producing a binary mask. Peripheral artifacts were removed using morphological erosion with a circular structuring element of radius 7 pixels (Fig. 4b).
Phantom mask after thresholding (a) and after erosion (b), a set of defined ROIs (c). Brain cross-section, three analyzed regions: white matter (yellow), cortex (green), CSF (blue) (d)

A set of non-overlapping circular ROIs (diameter 51 pixels, spacing 51 pixels) was created within the phantom mask, starting from the top-left corner ROIs fully contained within this area were retained, and the central ROI was positioned such that its centroid coincided with the centroid of the phantom cross-section on each slice (Fig. 4c). Each ROI comprised 2041 pixels, ensuring stable estimation of texture features, and was exported in a MaZda-compatible format. This procedure was applied to all 26 axial slices of the phantom acquisition, and resulted in 9 ROIs per slice. The total of 234 ROIs refers to one complete acquisition of the phantom (26 slices × 9 ROIs per slice). This ROI-generation procedure was repeated independently for all 14 acquisition configurations shown in Table 1.

Brain images were converted to NIfTI format and segmented using SynthSeg v2.0. Masks were resampled to the native spatial resolution using nearest-neighbor interpolation (SimpleITK v2.4.1). Three brain structures-white matter, cortex, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-were extracted and saved in a MaZda-compatible format. For the brain dataset, 39 cortical ROIs, 24 CSF ROIs, and 35 white-matter ROIs were defined on multiple axial slices, with separate ROIs placed in each slice and in both hemispheres wherever the respective tissue class was present. A sample brain cross-section with three marked regions of interest is shown in Fig. 4d. For each ROI (representing phantom and brain cross-sections), 570 texture features were determined for: intensity histogram, gradient map, Grey-level run-length matrices (GLRLM) built for horizontal, vertical, diagonal, and antidiagonal directions, grey-level co-occurrence matrices (GLCM) built for the same directions as GLRLM matrices and interpixel distance equal from 1 to 6, autoregressive model, Gabor filters, local binary patterns (over-complete, transition and center-symmetric), Haar discrete wavelet transform, and histogram of oriented gradients. For all ROIs, ±3s normalization was applied to reduce the impact of uneven brightness. Such normalization resulted also in ROI rescaling to an 8-bit intensity scale.

To assess the impact of reconstruction method and acceleration on image texture, the method described in was used. It examines how many texture features in images after AI reconstruction retain values like those obtained using the standard reconstruction method. Accelerated images were compared only to the corresponding non-accelerated reference reconstructed with the same method (standard or DR). Thus, GRAPPA and SMS acquisitions reconstructed with standard reconstruction were compared to the non-accelerated standard dataset, and the DR-reconstructed accelerated acquisitions were compared to the non-accelerated DR images. For every region of interest, a parameter vector was generated containing 570 texture features. For each pair, the Lin concordance correlation coefficient was calculated between the corresponding texture feature vectors:

where µ and σ are the mean values and standard deviations of the feature vector for the non-accelerated reference () and accelerated () acquisition, and ρ is the Pearson correlation coefficient between these vectors.
This coefficient was determined for each texture feature. We then checked how many features showed high correlation, considering three thresholds: 0.9, 0.8, and 0.7.

In addition, a Wilcoxon rank-sum test was performed on the texture feature vectors to test the null hypothesis (H0) that both vectors are samples from distributions with equal medians, compared to the alternative hypothesis that this is not the case. For each pair of images, the number of features that satisfy the H0 hypothesis was determined. The test was performed in MATLAB R2024b (MathWorks, Massachusetts, USA). A result was considered statistically significant for p < 0.05.
Results

The results obtained are shown in Table 3. For the phantom experiment, the non-accelerated acquisitions (without GRAPPA or SMS) were used as reference datasets for each reconstruction type (standard and Deep Resolve). For each accelerated protocol, we compared the texture feature vectors from the accelerated acquisition to the corresponding non-accelerated reference acquisition reconstructed with the same method (standard or DR). Table 3 contains the numbers of texture features with high correlation in relation to images reconstructed using standard methods and DR for three accepted correlation coefficient thresholds. It also provides the number of features that satisfy the hypothesis of equal medians of texture feature vectors (Wilcoxon test, a significance level of 0.05 was used).

Numbers of the best-correlated features and the number of texture features satisfying hypothesis H0. Analysis for phantom images between non-accelerated images and image with acceleration strategies, reconstructed with standard and Deep Resolve methods. “Mostly preserved features” was defined using an arbitrary threshold of 80%

Both reconstruction methods substantially affected texture features regardless of acceleration strategies type. As shown in Table 3, only a few features with a correlation > 0.9 were observed for standard reconstruction images and GRAPPA acceleration strategies. The number of features correlating at > 0.8 is also limited (the best situation for images for GRAPPA). For the highest acceleration factors in both GRAPPA and SMS, no parameters correlated above 0.7, indicating marked variability in texture features. No significant differences in terms of texture feature stability have been observed between the two reconstruction methods. This is illustrated in Fig. 5, which shows the differences in the number of highly correlated texture features between DR and standard reconstruction methods. However, Fig. 5 illustrates that DR preserved a greater number of features with unchanged medians across most acceleration settings (red bars in Fig. 5). A positive value means that DR preserved more features than standard reconstruction; a negative value indicates fewer preserved features.

Differences in the number of preserved phantom texture features between Deep Resolve (DR) and standard (STD) reconstruction for T2- and FLAIR-weighted images with GRAPPA and SMS acceleration. Bars show DR − STD for three acceleration factors (2, 3, 4) and four stability criteria (Lin concordance correlation coefficient > 0.9, > 0.8, > 0.7, and Wilcoxon test H0 accepted). Positive values indicate that DR preserved more texture features than standard reconstruction; negative values indicate the opposite

The small number of stable (highly correlated) texture features is due to noise and artifacts introduced by acceleration strategies, as shown in Fig. 6. Pseudocolors were used to better visualize these phenomena. Noise is particularly visible in SMS acceleration strategies. Noise and artifacts generate additional texture, invisible in phantom images without acceleration. This additional “noisy texture” is responsible for changes in texture feature values compared to the non-accelerated image. This is confirmed by the values of selected texture features determined for the slices from Fig. 6, presented in Table 4; Fig. 7. These parameters reflect the variability and degree of texture disorder. GradVariance determines the variance of the image gradient distribution and is evaluated based on the gradient matrix. The remaining features, determined for GLCM, reflect the texture brightness range (Contrast), the degree of brightness level disorder (Entropy), and the variability of the texture brightness distribution (DifVariance). Increasing values of these features, visible with larger factors in both acceleration methods, indicate increasingly variable and “rough” texture when compared to the smooth and uniform phantom image. Interestingly, this effect is less visible with DR, where noise and artifacts are smaller than with the standard reconstruction method.

Pseudocolor T2-weighted phantom cross-sections reconstructed with the standard method (top row) and Deep Resolve (bottom row). Columns correspond to different acquisition settings: None, GRAPPA 2, GRAPPA 3, GRAPPA 4, SMS 2, SMS 3, SMS 4. All images are displayed with the same intensity window using a common color scale bar shown below the panels

Comparison of texture feature values between Standard and Deep Resolve reconstructions across different acceleration methods. Each group represents a specific acceleration configuration (None, GRAPPA 2–4, SMS 2–4), with paired bars for Standard (dark color) and Deep Resolve (light color). Bar color families indicate the type of acceleration: blue (None), purple (GRAPPA), and green (SMS). All values are normalised to the Standard reconstruction without acceleration, which is defined as 100%
Selected texture feature values for phantom images with different acceleration and reconstruction methods

It is also worth noting that the most stable features with respect to the influence of acceleration strategies for both the standard and DR-based reconstruction methods were GLCM-based. These features remained among the most stable across all GRAPPA and SMS acceleration settings. Features determined for the autoregressive model and gradient matrix also appeared occasionally. The preservation of stable GLCM parameter values with different MRI acquisition parameters is confirmed by the results of, which analyzed the consistency of textural features for water and tissue phantoms acquired with T1 and T2 sequences using different parameters. Furthermore, in, which examined the impact of different MR image reconstruction methods for brain tumor phantoms, it was shown that some GLCM features retain their values to a high degree regardless of the reconstruction method.
Brain images
For the brain images, the results for the three analyzed regions (white matter, cortex, and CSF) are presented in Table 5. It contains averaged data obtained for 4 subjects.

passages.text: Both reconstruction methods significantly affect texture feature values regardless of the type of acceleration, but to a lesser extent than in phantoms. SMS has the slightly greatest impact on texture change, as fewer texture features preserve high correlation after such acceleration strategies. Texture features are much more stable with GRAPPA acceleration strategies, particularly for lower factors. As shown in Table 5, the CSF and cortex regions are the least affected regions in terms of preserving texture feature values, whereas white matter is most affected (the fewest preserved features). When using DR instead of standard reconstruction, the effect of acceleration on texture feature stability is reduced for most brain regions and acceleration settings, as indicated by the higher number of stable features in the DR images. This is demonstrated by the differences in the number of stable texture features satisfying the H0 hypothesis between DR and standard reconstruction (Fig. 8). The values are shown for the three assumed correlation coefficients and for all analyzed acceleration methods. For most cases, DR reconstruction preserves a larger number of texture features, meaning it modifies the texture of the original image to a lesser extent than STD reconstruction. The most pronounced deviation from this pattern (but only for the highest correlation threshold 0.9) is observed in the WM and CSF regions for the SMS2 and SMS3 acceleration strategies, where DR results in fewer highly correlated features than STD (resulting negative difference in Fig. 8) while still increasing the number of features satisfying the H0 hypothesis. For the majority of acceleration strategies, the cortex is the most stable tissue to DR reconstruction. This may be related to the fact that the gray matter of the cerebral cortex is characterized by the richest and most distinct visible structure, so subtle changes induced by DR only alter this structure in a limited way, making them less visible in its textural features. Accordingly, this ROI has the largest number of preserved texture features. As in the phantom analysis, for all 4 subjects, GLCM features formed the largest group of stable parameters across reconstruction and acceleration settings, with additional contributions from GLRLM, Gabor, and LBP features.

Averaged differences along with SEMs in the number of preserved texture features between Deep Resolve (DR) and standard reconstruction (STD) for T2-weighted brain images across cortex, white matter and CSF. Values represent DR − STD for three Lin concordance thresholds (> 0.9, > 0.8, > 0.7) and for the Wilcoxon H0 acceptance count. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean across subjects. All panels share the same y-axis scale. Positive values indicate that DR preserved more texture features than STD; negative values indicate that STD preserved more

It is also worth noting the relatively large variation in the differences in the number of stable features (particularly for GRAPPA), reflected by the higher SEM values shown in Fig. 8. This variability likely reflects inter-subject anatomical differences. Unlike the homogeneous phantom used in the experimental part of this study, human brain tissue exhibits substantial structural heterogeneity, including variability in cortical thickness, white-matter organization, and cerebrospinal fluid distribution, which may influence the spatial distribution of MR signal intensities and the resulting texture patterns. Because radiomic features quantify spatial relationships between voxel intensities, such anatomical variability can lead to measurable differences in texture features across subjects, even when identical acquisition protocols are used. Similar inter-subject variability of MRI texture features has been reported previously in radiomics studies (32).

Unlike the homogeneous phantom, brain tissues exhibit a much richer and spatially heterogeneous structure, which results in inherently more complex image textures. Under these conditions, acceleration-related noise and artifacts are superimposed on an already structured background and therefore contribute proportionally less to the overall texture description. As a result, the modification of texture feature values in brain tissue is markedly smaller than in the phantom, where the same artifacts introduce new high-frequency components that dominate the texture extracted from the otherwise uniform image.
Discussion
Advances in deep learning reconstruction and assessment limits

Recent progress in deep learning-based reconstruction has changed the way MRI is performed by allowing shorter scan times, better signal-to-noise ratio, and reduced motion artifacts. Several vendors have now incorporated DL into clinical products, including Siemens (Deep Resolve), GE (AIR Recon DL), Philips (SmartSpeed), and Fujifilm/Hitachi (Synergy DLR). Despite technical differences, all aim to deliver faster and more reliable imaging.

The present study examines Deep Resolve with a specific focus on texture features. We assessed its effect on stability of phantom and in vivo brain textures under GRAPPA and SMS acceleration schemes, with texture-based metrics for a more detailed evaluation than conventional SNR or CNR. The goal is to understand whether DL reconstruction can be introduced into quantitative imaging workflows in a way that maintains the stability of texture features.
While prior evaluations of DR have primarily relied on subjective image quality scoring and basic SNR or CNR metrics, these approaches may overlook subtle but diagnostically relevant image changes.
Comparative findings in phantom and brain imaging

A major finding of our study is the differential impact of DR on phantom versus brain images. Phantom data, characterized by structural homogeneity, were more susceptible to noise artifacts introduced by acceleration methods. This caused instability in texture features, especially at higher acceleration. Despite this, in the phantom experiment, DR improved the number of texture features that did not differ by the median (Wilcoxon test), indicating reduced distortion compared to standard reconstructions.

In contrast, brain tissues showed greater resilience to texture degradation due to their inherently diverse structure. The same noisy textures introduced by acceleration strategies had a comparatively smaller impact, modifying the original texture features to a lesser degree. Across all brain regions analyzed-cortex, cerebrospinal fluid, and white matter-DR preserved or enhanced the stability of texture features, particularly under moderate acceleration (e.g., SMS3 or GRAPPA R = 2).

The uniform spherical phantom was included as a standardized reference with highly homogeneous signal properties, allowing changes caused solely by reconstruction or acceleration to be detected more clearly. In such a controlled environment, even subtle alterations introduced by GRAPPA, SMS, or DR become easier to identify. However, the clinical relevance of these findings must be verified in structured biological tissue; therefore, we performed a parallel analysis using in vivo brain images. This combination enabled us to distinguish methodological effects from their anatomical implications. The observed differences confirm that results from homogeneous phantoms cannot be directly extrapolated to real anatomical structures. While uniform phantoms support standardization and baseline evaluation, more anatomically or texturally complex phantoms may be required to fully characterize AI-related artifacts and their clinical significance.
Deep resolve in the context of prior radiomic studies

Our results closely align with findings by Moummad et al., who demonstrated that deep learning reconstruction substantially reduced radiomic distortion introduced by conventional acceleration, lowering instability from ~ 80% to ~ 46%. We found DR (4 subject study) increased average stable features by up to 241 in cortex, 245 in CSF, and 211 in white matter. This corresponds to an increase of 42%, 43% and 37% in the total number of texture features (570) for these areas respectively. Altogether, it also raised the average number of features without median differences by up to 289 in cortex, 219 in CSF, and 283 in white matter. This suggests that DR not only improves visual quality but also helps maintain quantitative texture stability essential for downstream radiomic applications. Similarly, Clodi et al. demonstrated high agreement in hippocampal subfield segmentation between DL and conventional T2-weighted imaging, supporting the role of DL in preserving textural fidelity.

However, these results may differ for specific cases due to the relatively large standard deviation in the number of stable features, which likely reflects inter-individual anatomical variability between patients. Human brain tissue is characterized by substantial structural heterogeneity, including differences in cortical thickness, gyral patterns, white-matter organization, and cerebrospinal fluid distribution. Because texture features quantify spatial relationships between voxel intensities, such anatomical variability can significantly influence radiomic feature values even when identical acquisition parameters are used. This effect has been widely recognized in radiomics research. In particular, the Image Biomarker Standardization Initiative (IBSI) emphasizes that radiomic features are inherently dependent on the spatial structure and intensity distribution of the underlying tissue and may therefore vary across individuals due to biological and anatomical differences. Furthermore, Lambin and colleagues highlighted that radiomic features capture tissue phenotype and microstructural heterogeneity, meaning that variations in anatomy or tissue composition between subjects can lead to measurable differences in extracted imaging biomarkers. Variability in the number of preserved texture features observed in the present study should not be interpreted solely as methodological instability but also as a manifestation of natural biological variability between subjects, which is expected in in-vivo radiomic analyses.

Importantly, our study expands the understanding of how deep learning reconstruction affects radiomics. While Moummad et al. and others observed deep learning’s benefits in restoring corrupted features, our analysis quantifies how Siemens’ DR specifically stabilizes texture features across different tissues and acceleration schemes. Notably, CSF showed the highest numbers of features satisfying the Wilcoxon test, whereas cortex generally showed the highest numbers of preserved features at the Lin concordance thresholds; white matter was usually the least stable region. This suggests tissue-dependent effects, with implications for protocol acceleration strategies in clinical practice.

In addition, we observed that GLCM-derived features were the most robust across methods. These features are widely used in radiomics for tissue classification, treatment response prediction, and segmentation. For example, SumAverage has been used to assess bone aging in CT images, and InvDiffMoment has distinguished symptomatic vs. asymptomatic median nerve textures in ultrasound. DR’s preservation of such features supports clinical relevance and texture-based analysis.

Texture features are increasingly used as quantitative biomarkers in downstream analysis tasks, where their stability is critical. For example, Nurzyńska et al. showed that GLCM-derived features from vertebral CT images correlate with bone age and enable age prediction, implying that perturbations of the underlying texture would be expected to degrade model performance. Similarly, Hesse et al. highlighted that cardiac MR texture-based heterogeneity biomarkers may facilitate early detection of myocardial disease, risk stratification and prediction of treatment response, as they capture subtle spatial intensity variations that precede overt visual abnormalities. In such settings, reconstruction- or acceleration-induced texture changes would directly influence the derived biomarkers and could impair the detection of early or subclinical disease. Although the present study does not directly evaluate diagnostic tasks such as lesion detection, classification, or segmentation, texture-feature stability remains a necessary prerequisite for reliable radiomic biomarker development. In this context, our findings indicate that reconstruction and acceleration settings may substantially influence quantitative texture measurements and therefore should be carefully considered in radiomic and machine-learning applications.

White et al. conducted a multi-patient study (n = 58) of rectal MRI using both 1.5 T and 3 T systems, the authors compared AI-based reconstruction (via the same vendor’s deep-learning framework) to conventional T2-weighted imaging and assessed a suite of image-quality metrics (IQMs). They found that paired (full-reference) IQMs such as peak signal-to-noise ratio (pSNR), structural similarity index (SSIM), visual information fidelity (VIF), and spectral angle mapper (SAM) were highly sensitive to perturbations of the reconstruction pipeline-detecting deviations outside ± 2 standard deviations of the reference dataset. In contrast, unpaired (no-reference) metrics, including textural entropy and Tenengrad edge-strength, showed markedly lower sensitivity (p < 0.0379 for unpaired metrics, and p > 0.99 for paired metrics across field strengths).

This result is highly relevant to our work, as it underscores that while classical metrics of signal and structural similarity are effective at detecting reconstruction-related alterations, texture-based features may be comparatively less responsive to such changes. Our findings-showing differential stability of texture features under acceleration and DL reconstruction-align with the observation by White et al. that textural features may present less sensitivity than pSNR, SSIM or VIF. This emphasizes the need to not assume that texture features will behave identically to conventional IQMs when using AI reconstructions and quantify feature stability explicitly, rather than infer it indirectly.
Limitations and future directions

This study has several important limitations. First, the analysis was based on only 4 volunteers and one homogeneous spherical phantom, which substantially limits the generalisability of the findings. No repeated scans were acquired, preventing a formal assessment of intra-session or inter-session repeatability. Prior work has shown that many radiomic features are highly sensitive to acquisition and reconstruction variability, and that repeated measurements are essential for quantifying this variability, as demonstrated by Shur et al. and Baeßler et al..

Second, all imaging was performed on a single 1.5T Siemens MAGNETOM Altea scanner using a 16-channel head/neck coil and the vendor-specific Deep Resolve reconstruction pipeline. Therefore, the results cannot be extrapolated to other hardware platforms, field strengths, coil configurations, or other deep-learning reconstruction frameworks (e.g., GE AIR Recon DL, Philips SmartSpeed, Fujifilm Synergy DLR). The study also included only two 2D TSE sequences (T2-weighted and FLAIR), acquired under controlled, motion-free conditions. Texture-feature stability may differ in other anatomies, clinical scenarios with motion, and sequences such as 3D TSE, gradient-echo, diffusion-weighted, or perfusion imaging.

Third, the limited number of images limits the statistical certainty of the observed trends. Although the combined phantom-brain approach allowed us to separate methodological effects from anatomical effects, the findings should be interpreted as preliminary. More complex or anatomically accurate phantoms may be useful to further characterise AI-related artifacts, and larger in vivo datasets are essential to confirm tissue-specific behaviour of texture features.

Finally, while Deep Resolve reduced certain noise-related distortions, it did not fully eliminate texture variability, particularly at high acceleration factors. Future studies should evaluate whether the texture modifications introduced by DL reconstruction impact the performance of downstream radiomic tasks such as tissue classification, lesion detection, and segmentation.

Overall, this work provides an initial investigation of texture-feature stability in DL-reconstructed images, but broader validation across scanners, sequences, anatomies, and repeated acquisitions is required before general clinical conclusions can be drawn.
